brief:
TOPIC: Catheter ablation of AF and its effect on cognitive function.

KNOWLEDGE GAP / WHY IT MATTERS: Early postoperative cognitive dysfunction (POCD) has been reported after AF ablation; whether such cognitive decline persists over the long term was unknown. Need to know if ablation causes lasting cognitive harm.

OBJECTIVE: Determine whether AF catheter ablation is associated with persistent cognitive dysfunction at 12-month follow-up.

DESIGN/SETTING: Prospective randomized controlled trial.

POPULATION/SAMPLE: 100 patients with symptomatic AF who had failed at least 1 antiarrhythmic drug; randomized. 96 participants completed the protocol. Mean age 59 +/- 12 years; 32% women; 46% had persistent AF.

INTERVENTION/COMPARISON: Randomized to either AF catheter ablation OR ongoing medical therapy; followed 12 months. Cognitive performance assessed via 6 cognitive tests at baseline and at 3, 6, and 12 months.

KEY RESULTS (with numbers):
- Prevalence of NEW cognitive dysfunction, ablation arm vs medical arm:
  * 3 months: 14% vs 2%; P = 0.03 (significant)
  * 6 months: 4% vs 2%; P = NS
  * 12 months: 0% vs 2%; P = NS
- Ablation time was an independent predictor of POCD (P = 0.03).
- Significant IMPROVEMENT in cognitive scores at 12 months seen in 14% of ablation-arm patients vs 0% (no patients) in medical arm; P = 0.007.

MAIN TAKEAWAY: POCD does occur after AF ablation but is transient, with complete recovery by 12-month follow-up; no persistent long-term cognitive dysfunction. A subset even showed cognitive improvement at 12 months.
